# Figure description: скв_169.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/raw_graphics/Колонки_ скважин/скв_169.jpg`

---

## Figure Type

This is a **composite borehole/well log (геологический разрез скважины)** — a detailed lithological and geophysical well log sheet typical of Soviet-era exploration drilling documentation. It combines a lithological column, multiple geophysical logging curves (likely gamma-ray, resistivity, spontaneous potential, and/or neutron logs), and tabular data columns on the right side. A smaller auxiliary diagram appears at the bottom of the sheet.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The figure documents a single deep exploration borehole (скважина), drilled as part of the regional survey of northern Kazakhstan (map sheets M-42 / N-42). The header area contains the well identifier, location coordinates, and survey metadata. The log appears to cover a substantial depth range — likely **0 to approximately 1,000–1,200 meters** based on the length of the column and tick-mark spacing visible along the left margin. The lithological column on the far left shows multiple stratigraphic intervals. The title area at the top reads something approximating **"СУБЛНИЕ"** or a similar abbreviated heading (possibly "СУБЛИНЕНИЕ" — a technical term), with "ПОЛЕ" and other text suggesting a field designation. Formation names and depth markers are noted along the left side of the lithological column.

---

## Key Content

**Lithological Column (far left):**
- Multiple distinct lithological units are shown using standard Soviet geological symbols:
  - **Hatched/diagonal patterns** at the top — likely representing surficial sediments, clays, or loose cover
  - **Dotted patterns** — likely sandstone or sandy intervals
  - **Brick/block patterns** — likely carbonate rocks (limestone or dolomite)
  - **Horizontal line patterns** — likely argillites or shales
  - **Cross-hatched intervals** — possibly crystalline basement or intrusive rocks
- The column shows gradual transitions and sharp contacts between units, suggesting unconformities or lithological boundaries of stratigraphic significance

**Geophysical Curves (central portion — the dominant visual element):**
- Two or three overlapping logging curves occupy the central panel, spanning the full depth range
- The curves show high-amplitude, high-frequency deflections through the middle section of the log (approximately 200–800 m depth), indicating **heterogeneous, interbedded lithologies** — consistent with alternating resistive and conductive beds
- The **upper portion** (shallow depths) shows relatively flat, low-amplitude curves — consistent with uniform cover sediments
- The **lower portion** shows progressively lower-amplitude, smoother curves — possibly indicating more homogeneous deeper units (basement?)
- A notable **broad, high-amplitude excursion** is visible in the mid-section, which may indicate a porous/permeable interval, a mineralized zone, or a fluid-bearing horizon
- What appears to be a **resistivity or PS (spontaneous potential) curve** on the right side of the central panel shows sympathetic but distinct behavior from the left curve, useful for lithological discrimination

**Right-side Data Table:**
- A large tabular section on the right side of the sheet contains numerical data organized in rows corresponding to depth intervals
- Columns likely record: core recovery (%), lithological description codes, physical property measurements, and possibly mineralogical or geochemical assay results
- Many cells appear sparsely filled, suggesting intermittent core recovery or selective sampling

**Bottom Auxiliary Diagram:**
- A smaller log or cross-section appears at the bottom of the sheet, possibly showing a **summary correlation panel** or an enlarged section of a particular depth interval of interest
- It includes its own scale and curve traces

  - **"Выход керна"** — Core recovery

  - **"Мощность"** — Thickness

## Research Value

This composite well log provides a direct record of subsurface lithology, stratigraphy, and physical properties at a specific drilling location within the survey area, allowing correlation between geophysical anomalies identified in regional gravity/magnetic surveys and actual rock types encountered at depth. The high-amplitude geophysical responses in the mid-section of the log are particularly significant for identifying potential mineralized or fluid-bearing horizons that may be associated with the mineral targets of the 1983–1984 survey. For an AI research assistant or geologist, this figure answers the question: *"What lithologies and physical property contrasts exist at depth at this location, and do they correspond to economically significant intervals?"* — making it a critical ground-truth dataset for calibrating the regional geophysical interpretations described elsewhere in Report 36572.
